description: A polar vortex event drove temperatures to -12°F in Burlington County, New Jersey, causing heating load in the PJME-NJ zone to spike by 3,800 MW above seasonal norms. Grid operators issued an Emergency Energy Alert and activated 1,400 MW of interruptible load contracts with industrial customers along the Route 1 corridor in central New Jersey. Wind chill values reached -28°F in Atlantic County, sustaining demand 31% above the 10-year average for 34 consecutive hours before a moderation allowed controlled load restoration.

The June 2012 derecho struck the Mid-Atlantic corridor with sustained winds of 82 mph, severing 500 kV transmission towers along the Bedington-Doubs corridor in western Maryland and instantly removing 2,900 MW of capacity from the PJME-MD zone. Over 1.1 million customers in Montgomery and Prince George's Counties lost power as the Rockville and Germantown substations dropped offline within 47 seconds of the initial line fault. Mutual aid crews mobilized from Virginia and Pennsylvania worked continuously for 58 hours to restore transmission path integrity and re-energize distribution feeders across the Washington suburbs.

A heat dome settled over the Chesapeake Bay region, pushing temperatures to 107°F at Reagan National Airport and driving PJME-DC zone peak demand to 7,200 MW, a record for early August. Operators issued a Conservative Operations advisory and dispatched 1,650 MW of combustion turbine capacity from the Chalk Point and Morgantown facilities in southern Maryland to prevent voltage collapse on the 230 kV network feeding downtown Washington. Demand remained 26% above the seasonal norm for 18 hours as overnight temperatures failed to drop below 84°F, preventing load recovery until a frontal passage early on August 5th.

Hurricane Sandy made landfall near Atlantic City, New Jersey with sustained winds of 90 mph and a storm surge of 9.2 feet, flooding 345 kV substations in Hudson and Essex Counties and removing 5,400 MW of load-serving capacity from the PJME-NJ zone in under two hours. Flooding at the Linden substation caused cascading faults that isolated 780,000 customers in northeastern New Jersey and required manual restoration sequencing from the PJM control room in Valley Forge over a 72-hour period. Saltwater intrusion in underground cable vaults in lower Manhattan extended full system restoration well beyond initial estimates, requiring replacement of 14 cable sections totaling 8.3 circuit miles.

A natural gas pressure curtailment on the Transco Zone 6 pipeline serving the Delaware Valley reduced firm gas supply to the Edgewood and Marcus Hook combined-cycle plants in Delaware by 1,200 MW of available capacity during a period of moderate winter load. PJME-PA-EAST operators compensated by increasing output from the Limerick Nuclear Generating Station by 180 MW to its licensed maximum and importing 600 MW across the PJM-PSEG interface from New Jersey. The curtailment lasted 22 hours before pipeline pressure was restored following a compressor station repair near Lambertville, New Jersey.

An Arctic air mass pushed temperatures to -8°F in Wilmington, Delaware, causing natural gas distribution pressure to fall as residential heating demand overwhelmed pipeline capacity across the Delmarva Peninsula. PJME-MD operators activated dual-fuel capability at the Indian River and Vienna generating stations in Maryland, switching 1,100 MW of capacity to distillate oil within three hours of the low-pressure advisory from Eastern Shore Natural Gas. Load in the DelMarVa control area peaked at 4,100 MW, exceeding the winter design peak by 9% and requiring emergency energy purchases from neighboring utilities totaling 380 MW.

Winter Storm Nemo deposited 28 inches of snow across Bergen and Passaic Counties in New Jersey, triggering wind turbine icing shutdowns at the Bayonne Energy Center and reducing available capacity in the PJME-NJ zone by 940 MW overnight. Transmission line ice loading on the 230 kV Roseland-Hillside corridor caused protective relays to operate at 03:47 EST, requiring operators to reroute 1,300 MW of power flow through the alternate Sewaren-Kearny path at an elevated loading of 112% of normal emergency rating. Full system restoration was completed after 29 hours once de-icing crews cleared switching stations in Monmouth County and the Roseland corridor was returned to service.

A week-long heat event across the Delmarva Peninsula kept temperatures at 99°F in Dover, Delaware, driving residential cooling load in the PJME-MD zone 18% above forecast and forcing operators to activate 620 MW of demand response resources. The Baltimore Gas and Electric Direct Load Control program cycled air conditioning units for approximately 340,000 residential customers across Anne Arundel and Howard Counties for four-hour intervals throughout the peak period. Peak demand in the PJME-MD zone reached 11,400 MW at 16:15 EST on July 19th, coming within 380 MW of the zone's all-time record set in August 2006.

Scheduled maintenance on the 500 kV Carson-Possum Point transmission line in Prince William County, Virginia required a planned 48-hour outage window to replace 12 suspension insulators damaged by UV degradation on Tower 47 through Tower 59 along the Quantico easement. PJME-VA operators coordinated with Dominion Virginia Power to maintain N-1 contingency compliance by pre-positioning an additional 400 MW of spinning reserve at the Possum Point and Birchwood combustion turbine facilities. All switching operations were completed safely and the line was returned to service 6 hours ahead of the planned 54-hour window with no customer interruptions recorded.

A pre-Thanksgiving ice storm deposited 0.75 inches of glaze ice on conductors across northern Virginia and the Maryland suburbs of Washington, causing protective relay operations on 14 distribution feeders in Fairfax and Loudoun Counties and affecting 87,000 customers in the PJME-VA zone. Ice-induced galloping on 138 kV lines between the Ox and Loudoun substations tripped protective relays twice within a six-hour window, requiring operators to manually reroute 850 MW of load flow through the Doubs-Loudoun path until temperatures rose above freezing. Field crews completed ice patrolling and necessary hardware replacements within 38 hours, restoring all high-voltage facilities to normal configuration ahead of the holiday travel peak.

The January 2014 polar vortex event drove wind chills to -35°F in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, causing natural gas supply pressure on Tennessee Gas Pipeline Zone 6 to fall critically and forcing emergency fuel switching at 2,300 MW of gas-fired generation in the PJME-PA-EAST zone. PJM declared a Maximum Generation Emergency at 06:00 EST and called upon all available capacity including 480 MW of demand response resources enrolled in the Emergency Load Response Program across Chester and Delaware Counties. The Limerick Nuclear Generating Station and Exelon's Eddystone oil-fired units provided critical voltage support during peak morning load of 16,200 MW before temperatures moderated enough to allow gas supply restoration after 44 hours.

Winter Storm Pax delivered 14 inches of snow followed by a quarter-inch ice glaze across Baltimore and Annapolis, causing 23 transmission line trips on the BGE 115 kV network in Baltimore County and Anne Arundel County within a four-hour window between 21:00 and 01:00 EST. PJME-MD zone operators transferred 1,100 MW of generation dispatch to the Calvert Cliffs Nuclear Power Plant and the Morgantown oil and gas plant to compensate for import capability reductions on the iced northern corridors. Restoration of all transmission facilities required 47 hours, with the final line at the Northbrook substation in Baltimore County returned to service at 20:15 EST on February 15th.

FERC Order 745 compliance implementation required PJM to revise demand response compensation rules for the PJME-NJ zone, triggering a review of 1,840 MW of enrolled emergency demand response capacity across 412 commercial and industrial customers in Middlesex, Union, and Hudson Counties. The compliance filing required operators to re-register 14 curtailment service providers and retest baseline methodologies for 68 large commercial accounts including data centers in Newark and Edison that collectively represent 340 MW of registered curtailment capacity. No operational impacts were observed during the compliance period, but administrative processing delayed summer demand response enrollment confirmations by approximately three weeks for affected customers.

A heat advisory with temperatures reaching 101°F at Philadelphia International Airport drove PJME-PA-EAST zone peak demand to 14,800 MW, triggering deployment of 1,200 MW of demand response resources through PECO Energy's commercial load curtailment program across Montgomery and Bucks Counties. PJM operators issued a Hot Weather Alert at 09:00 EST and requested all generators in eastern Pennsylvania to maximize output, bringing the Limerick Nuclear station to its full licensed capacity of 2,317 MW and dispatching the Conowingo hydroelectric facility at 480 MW. Demand remained elevated for 11 hours above the 14,000 MW threshold before an approaching cold front provided sufficient cooling to reduce air conditioning load by late evening.

An unplanned forced outage at the Peach Bottom Atomic Power Station Unit 3 in York County, Pennsylvania removed 1,339 MW of baseload capacity from the PJME-PA-EAST zone following a reactor trip caused by a feedwater flow instrument anomaly at 08:17 EST. PJM operators responded by increasing economic dispatch of natural gas combined-cycle units at the Calpine Bethlehem Energy Center and the Sunbury generating station by a combined 820 MW within 30 minutes of the trip notification. Unit 3 was returned to 100% power after 31 hours following inspection of the feedwater instrumentation and satisfactory completion of NRC pre-startup review requirements at the York Haven control point.

An early-season lake-effect snowstorm combined with a sharp Arctic cold front drove temperatures to 12°F in northern Delaware, generating a demand spike of 1,800 MW above the day-ahead forecast in the PJME-MD zone as heating systems activated simultaneously across New Castle County. Operators at the PJM control room requested emergency energy from the APS zone via the AP-South interface, importing 680 MW to supplement local generation and maintain area control error within acceptable limits during the evening peak. The storm produced 8 inches of snow in Newark, Delaware and 11 inches in Elkton, Maryland, causing 14 distribution relay operations that interrupted service to 41,000 customers for an average of 4.2 hours before full restoration.

Winter Storm Juno tracked along the New Jersey coast with wind gusts reaching 65 mph at Cape May and 22 inches of snow accumulation in Ocean County, causing 18 transmission-level protective relay operations across the PJME-NJ southern network between midnight and 06:00 EST. The Tuckerton and Cape May substations were isolated by cascading faults on 230 kV lines, requiring manual sectionalizing operations that affected 210,000 customers in Atlantic, Cape May, and Ocean Counties for periods ranging from 3 to 41 hours. PSE&G and Atlantic City Electric mutual aid teams completed transmission restoration within 52 hours, though distribution system repairs in beach communities on Long Beach Island extended full customer restoration to 68 hours.

Sustained temperatures of -4°F in Fairfax County, Virginia, representing the coldest February 21st reading in 28 years, drove PJME-VA zone heating load to 13,900 MW and triggered emergency fuel switching at 940 MW of natural gas peaking units to distillate oil reserves at the Possum Point and Loudoun combustion turbine facilities. Dominion Virginia Power activated interruptible service contracts with 47 large commercial and industrial customers in Northern Virginia representing 620 MW of load curtailment, including data center operators in Ashburn and Reston who shifted non-critical computing loads to off-peak scheduling. PJM maintained adequate reserves throughout the event but issued a conservative operations advisory for 14 hours as reserve margins narrowed to within 850 MW of the minimum threshold during the 07:00 EST morning peak.

A planned 72-hour maintenance outage on the 500 kV Claytor-Mount Storm transmission line in the Appalachian Mountains of western Virginia required pre-coordination of generation redispatch in the PJME-VA zone, adding approximately $340,000 to congestion costs over the outage window. PJM's transmission operations team arranged for Dominion Virginia Power to increase output at the North Anna Nuclear Power Station by 180 MW and pre-position 300 MW of fast-start generation at the Bremo Bluff and Possum Point facilities to maintain contingency reserve requirements during the outage. The line was successfully returned to service on April 17th following replacement of 23 suspension clamps and 8 damper assemblies on the 138-mile Appalachian corridor, with zero safety incidents and all work completed within the authorized outage window.

Above-normal temperatures reaching 97°F at BWI Airport drove PJME-MD zone peak demand to 10,800 MW, exceeding the June forecast peak by 1,400 MW and prompting BGE to activate 520 MW of demand response across its Smart Energy Rewards program participants in Baltimore and Howard Counties. PJM operators coordinated emergency energy imports of 480 MW across the Allegheny-Maryland interface to supplement local gas and oil peaking capacity, while the Calvert Cliffs Nuclear Power Plant maintained full dual-unit output of 1,790 MW throughout the event. Temperature-humidity index values above 105°F sustained elevated cooling load until a weak cold frontal passage at approximately 21:00 EST reduced demand by 1,200 MW within two hours, ending the emergency condition 8 hours after its declaration.

A transformer failure at the Oyster Creek Nuclear Generating Station in Ocean County, New Jersey caused an unplanned shutdown of the 619 MW unit at 12:47 EST, reducing PJME-NJ available capacity during a period when afternoon demand was approaching 18,400 MW under 98°F temperatures at Newark Liberty International Airport. PSE&G invoked emergency load curtailment agreements with 34 large industrial accounts in the Newark and Elizabeth industrial corridors, shedding approximately 480 MW of interruptible load within 18 minutes of the plant trip notification to prevent a voltage emergency on the 230 kV Newark area network. System conditions stabilized within 90 minutes as PJM dispatched additional combined-cycle capacity from the Linden and Bayonne generating stations, and the PJME-NJ reserve margin was restored to above minimum thresholds by 15:30 EST.

Remnants of Hurricane Joaquin produced 12.4 inches of rainfall in 24 hours across central New Jersey, with wind gusts reaching 54 mph at Trenton-Mercer Airport and causing 22 distribution feeder relay operations across Mercer and Monmouth Counties in the PJME-NJ zone. The Manasquan River crested at 11.8 feet above flood stage, inundating the Lakehurst distribution substation in Ocean County and requiring an emergency de-energization of four 69 kV feeders serving the Toms River area to prevent equipment damage. Restoration was completed within 39 hours once floodwaters receded sufficiently to allow crew access and inspection of submerged switchgear at the Lakehurst facility, with temporary portable substations deployed by JCP&L serving 12,000 customers during the extended repair period.

Winter Storm Jonas blanketed the Washington DC metro area with 28.4 inches of snow at Reagan National Airport, the largest snowstorm on record, grounding all road traffic for 36 hours and triggering a sustained 3,200 MW demand increase in the PJME-DC zone as buildings ran heating systems continuously in near-zero wind chills. PJM declared a Maximum Generation Emergency at 19:00 EST on January 22nd and activated all available combustion turbine capacity including 840 MW from the Dickerson and Potomac River generating stations in Montgomery and Prince George's Counties. Emergency operations continued for 52 hours as road closures prevented fuel truck deliveries to several distillate-fired peaking units, requiring coordinated fuel logistics management between Pepco, PEPCO Holdings, and PJM operations staff to maintain generation output.

Temperatures fell to -11°F at the Philadelphia International Airport weather station on February 15th, the coldest reading in the PJME-PA-EAST zone in 21 years, causing gas distribution pressure on the Elizabethtown Gas network to fall below minimum operating pressure and triggering automatic generator fuel switching at the Martins Creek and Portland generating stations totaling 1,400 MW. PECO Energy activated its curtailment service provider network, reducing load by 780 MW from 186 commercial and industrial accounts in Philadelphia, Montgomery, and Chester Counties through a four-hour conservation voltage reduction event coordinated with PJM dispatch. Peak demand in the PJME-PA-EAST zone reached 15,600 MW at 08:45 EST, the highest January-February peak recorded for the zone since 2003, before a gradual warming trend allowed restoration of gas pressure and deactivation of emergency protocols after 41 hours.

Implementation of FERC Order 825 compliance requirements for inertia and frequency response obligations prompted PJM to revise operating reserve requirements for the PJME-MD zone, increasing minimum online spinning reserve obligations from 1,400 MW to 1,680 MW to account for the reduced inertia contribution from retired coal generation at the Chalk Point and Brandon Shores facilities. Affected generating units, including the Calvert Cliffs Nuclear Station and several BGE-contracted gas peakers, were notified of revised dispatch parameters that would increase their frequency response obligations by an average of 12 MW per unit during peak load periods. The rule change resulted in estimated annual compliance costs of approximately $2.1 million for PJME-MD zone capacity providers, with no operational disruptions recorded during the implementation period.

An intense heat wave with temperatures of 103°F at Dulles International Airport drove PJME-VA zone peak demand to 15,100 MW, within 400 MW of the all-time zone record, and forced PJM to issue a Conservative Operations Advisory at 13:00 EST requesting all online generators to maximize output and defer planned maintenance activities. Dominion Virginia Power's demand response portfolio for Northern Virginia, including 28 large data center and federal government facility accounts in Fairfax and Arlington Counties representing 740 MW, was fully activated under interruptible service agreements for four consecutive hours during the peak period. The combination of demand response activation, emergency imports from the PJM-APS zone, and full commitment of 580 MW of combustion turbine capacity at the Possum Point facility maintained reserve margins above the minimum threshold throughout the 15:00-19:00 EST critical window.

Scheduled replacement of bus work and disconnect switches at the Claymont 230 kV substation in northern Delaware required a planned 24-hour outage that reduced available transformation capacity in the PJME-MD zone by 600 MW and required pre-positioning of 280 MW of additional fast-start generation at the Dover Air Force Base emergency generation facility and the Hay Road combined-cycle plant. Delmarva Power coordinated with PJM transmission operations to schedule the work during a moderate-load late-summer weekend window with forecast peak demand of 7,400 MW, providing 1,800 MW of headroom below the zone's emergency operating limit. Work was completed 3 hours ahead of schedule due to favorable weather conditions, and the substation was returned to service on September 2nd with new equipment rated to carry 850 MW of continuous load under summer emergency conditions.

A natural gas pipeline rupture on the Tennessee Gas Pipeline Zone 6 mainline near Princeton, New Jersey caused a 40% reduction in firm gas delivery capacity to combined-cycle generating units in central New Jersey, forcing the Sewaren and Bayway generating stations to reduce output by a combined 980 MW during the late-afternoon demand peak. PJME-NJ operators activated 420 MW of oil-capable dual-fuel generation at the Kearny and Edison facilities within 90 minutes of the pipeline isolation event and coordinated emergency energy imports of 340 MW across the NYISO-PJME interface to maintain adequate voltage on the 230 kV network serving Newark and Elizabeth. Pipeline repair crews from Tennessee Gas Pipeline restored mainline pressure after 33 hours of emergency excavation and repair work near the Kingston junction east of Princeton, allowing gas-fired units to return to full output by 03:00 EST on December 2nd.

Arctic temperatures of -6°F in Baltimore combined with peak residential heating demand drove PJME-MD zone load to 12,100 MW at 07:00 EST, triggering an Operating Procedure 4 action by BGE to reduce feeder voltage by 3% across 68 distribution circuits in Baltimore City and Baltimore County, saving approximately 240 MW through conservation voltage reduction. Southern Maryland Electric Cooperative and SMECO's interruptible commercial customers were activated at 06:30 EST, shedding 180 MW from food distribution and cold storage facilities in Charles and Calvert Counties that had enrolled in BGE's commercial demand response program. Calvert Cliffs Nuclear Power Plant ran at full dual-unit output of 1,790 MW throughout the event while Morgantown and Chalk Point oil and gas facilities provided 1,100 MW of critical peak generation support before temperatures rose above 10°F by midday.

Winter Storm Stella produced 19 inches of snow at Philadelphia International Airport and 24 inches in Montgomery County, Pennsylvania, driving PJME-PA-EAST heating load 28% above the day-ahead forecast as temperatures hovered near 18°F throughout the event. Snow accumulation on distribution lines in Bucks and Chester Counties caused 34 feeder relay operations, interrupting service to 185,000 PECO Energy customers while PJM operators managed a sharp 2,600 MW morning ramp driven by electric heating and cooking load as residents sheltered in place during the storm. Transmission facilities performed well throughout the storm with only three protective operations on the 115 kV network, and all interrupted customers were restored within 48 hours as PECO deployed 1,800 crew members across the PJME-PA-EAST zone following the cessation of snowfall.

FERC's approval of PJM's proposed Capacity Performance rules required the PJME-NJ zone to increase its installed reserve margin from 16.5% to 18.2%, adding 1,340 MW of required capacity procurement and prompting PSE&G and JCP&L to initiate emergency capacity RFPs for peaking resources able to perform at design conditions during extreme weather events. Compliance reviews of 14 combustion turbine units enrolled in the Capacity Performance program identified 6 units in Atlantic and Monmouth Counties with inadequate fuel supply diversity, triggering $1.8 million in performance deficiency charges and requiring operators to file corrective action plans with PJM within 45 days of the rule's effective date. The regulatory implementation required 48 hours of operational planning coordination between PJME-NJ zone transmission owners and PJM's capacity markets group to finalize locational deliverability area boundaries and update resource accreditation factors for affected generating units.

Temperatures of 100°F at Reagan National Airport combined with a humidity-driven heat index of 113°F pushed PJME-DC zone peak demand to 7,480 MW at 15:45 EST, the highest daily peak recorded in the zone since July 2011, and triggered deployment of all 920 MW of Pepco's summer demand response portfolio across the District of Columbia and the Maryland suburbs. Federal government facilities in the District, operating under General Services Administration emergency conservation protocols, reduced building cooling loads by approximately 240 MW through thermostat setpoint adjustments across 180 federally managed properties in the Capitol Hill, Foggy Bottom, and Navy Yard areas. PJM emergency operations imported 380 MW across the Dominion-Pepco interface from Virginia to supplement local gas turbine capacity at the Benning Road and Buzzard Point facilities, maintaining voltage within acceptable limits on the downtown Washington 230 kV network throughout the peak window.

A scheduled maintenance forced outage at the Limerick Nuclear Generating Station Unit 1 in Montgomery County, Pennsylvania was extended by 16 hours due to discovery of unexpected wear on a reactor coolant pump bearing, temporarily removing 1,160 MW of baseload capacity from the PJME-PA-EAST zone during a mild autumn load period with demand of approximately 9,200 MW. PECO Energy and PJM coordinated an economic redispatch of 840 MW from Exelon's combined-cycle portfolio including the Grays Ferry and Schuylkill generating stations in Philadelphia to compensate for the reduced nuclear output without impacting area control error metrics. The extended forced outage added approximately $640,000 in incremental fuel costs to the zone's generation dispatch over the 16-hour overrun period, and Limerick Unit 1 was returned to full power on September 13th following satisfactory completion of NRC-required post-maintenance testing.

A historic cold wave following Christmas 2017 drove temperatures to -4°F in Richmond, Virginia and caused natural gas curtailments on the Dominion Energy Transmission system serving the PJME-VA zone, forcing emergency fuel switching at 1,800 MW of dual-fuel generating capacity across the Possum Point, Bremo Bluff, and Chesterfield generating stations in the greater Richmond area. PJM declared a Maximum Generation Emergency at 07:30 EST on December 27th and issued a public appeal for conservation across Virginia and the Washington DC metro area, requesting customers to reduce thermostat settings by 4°F and defer high-electricity-usage activities until after 21:00 EST. Reserve margins in the PJME-VA zone narrowed to 640 MW above the minimum threshold at 09:15 EST before emergency energy imports of 1,200 MW from the AEP zone via the Doubs-Mt. Storm interface stabilized the system, and conditions normalized after 36 hours as temperatures rose above 10°F by December 29th.

Winter Storm Grayson brought temperatures of -13°F to Dover, Delaware and -9°F to Salisbury, Maryland during the so-called bomb cyclone event, driving PJME-MD zone demand to 13,700 MW and exhausting all locally committed generation while natural gas pipelines experienced flow reductions of up to 35% on the Columbia Gas Transmission system serving eastern Maryland. PJM's system operations declared a Conservative Operations advisory at 04:00 EST and committed all available oil-fired generation at Chalk Point and Indian River at a combined output of 1,400 MW as emergency actions to prevent reserve deficiency during the 06:00-10:00 EST peak window when demand surpassed generation capacity by 1,100 MW. Emergency energy imports from NYISO and MISO totaling 1,900 MW prevented a controlled load shedding event, and the cold wave moderated after 48 hours as the system gradually returned to normal operating conditions by January 6th.

The same bomb cyclone event that affected PJME-MD also drove temperatures to -11°F at Philadelphia International Airport, generating PJME-PA-EAST zone demand of 16,800 MW and triggering fuel supply emergencies at 2,100 MW of gas-fired generation as Transco Zone 6 pipeline pressure dropped to 82% of contracted minimum delivery pressure due to statewide heating demand on the gas distribution system. PECO Energy activated all 1,340 MW of its Capacity Performance demand response portfolio within 45 minutes of PJM's emergency notification, curtailing load from 287 industrial and commercial accounts including major manufacturing facilities in Chester and Delaware Counties, while Limerick Nuclear ran at 102% licensed thermal power under NRC-approved stretch power uprate conditions. Full restoration of gas pipeline pressure required coordination between PJM, Transco, and the Pennsylvania Public Utility Commission over 44 hours, with the Limerick and Peach Bottom nuclear stations providing essential voltage support throughout the extended emergency period.

Nor'easter Riley struck the New Jersey coastline with wind gusts of 76 mph at Atlantic City International Airport and a storm surge of 5.8 feet at the Barnegat Light gauge, causing 28 transmission relay operations on the PJME-NJ 230 kV coastal network and isolating the Tuckerton substation from the system for 14 hours during the height of the storm. JCP&L's transmission operations team managed 18 simultaneous fault conditions across Ocean, Monmouth, and Atlantic Counties from their emergency operations center in Red Bank, coordinating with PJM to maintain N-2 contingency operations during the period of maximum system stress when 1,600 MW of coastal load was interrupted. Damage to the Mantoloking and Harvey Cedars switching stations on Long Beach Island required temporary portable substations to be deployed for 58 hours before permanent repairs could be completed on the storm-battered barrier island distribution infrastructure.

Nor'easter Quinn struck the Philadelphia region one week after Riley, depositing 21 inches of snow in Montgomery County, Pennsylvania and 17 inches in Chester County, causing 41 distribution relay operations on PECO Energy's 69 kV network across the Philadelphia suburbs and interrupting service to 210,000 customers in the PJME-PA-EAST zone. Snow loading caused a cascading failure of three 115 kV structures on the Whitpain-Valley Forge transmission corridor in Montgomery County, requiring PECO transmission engineers to establish an emergency switching configuration routing 1,400 MW of center-city Philadelphia supply through the alternate Plymouth Meeting-Swedesburg path at 98% of its emergency rating for 29 hours. Restoration of all transmission structures required specialized crane equipment that could not access the rural Whitpain Township easement until snowplowing operations cleared Route 202 and a private access road, extending the full repair timeline to 67 hours from the initial fault event.

A planned 48-hour outage of the Chalk Point-Bowie 230 kV transmission line in Prince George's County, Maryland for conductor replacement on a 12-mile section crossing the Patuxent River lowlands reduced available import capacity into the PJME-MD zone from the south by 900 MW and required BGE to pre-commit 480 MW of combustion turbine capacity at the Perryman and Wagner generating stations during the peak daily load hours. PJM transmission operators coordinated with Pepco Holdings to maintain N-1 contingency compliance by loading the alternate Chalk Point-Riva 138 kV path to 94% of its normal emergency rating, which required hourly ampacity checks and operator confirmation of conductor sag clearance given above-normal ambient temperatures of 72°F during the outage window. The conductor replacement project was completed within the authorized outage window with no operational incidents, and the line was returned to service on May 10th with a new conductor rated at 1,100 MW of continuous load capacity, a 22% improvement over the replaced conductor.

The summer solstice heat event drove temperatures to 98°F at Baltimore-Washington International Airport, generating a late-afternoon demand surge of 1,900 MW above the day-ahead BGE forecast as residential air conditioning load in the PJME-MD zone reached 87% of all distribution transformer capacity in Howard and Anne Arundel Counties. PJM operators issued a Hot Weather Alert at 10:00 EST requesting BGE to prepare demand response resources, and 640 MW of curtailment through the Smart Energy Rewards thermostat cycling program was activated at 13:30 EST covering 284,000 enrolled residential and small commercial accounts across the BGE service territory. The Calvert Cliffs Nuclear Power Plant operated at its maximum licensed output of 1,790 MW for 14 consecutive hours, providing critical baseload support as gas-fired peakers at the Gould Street and C.P. Crane facilities contributed an additional 480 MW of emergency capacity to maintain reserve margins above the required minimum throughout the peak period.

Independence Day holiday heat peaked at 102°F at Dulles International Airport, Virginia, with the PJME-VA zone experiencing 14,200 MW of demand during the 14:00-17:00 EST peak window due to the combination of residential cooling load and delayed occupancy from holiday schedules that prevented commercial buildings from pre-cooling overnight. Dominion Energy Virginia activated 860 MW of demand response from its largest commercial and data center accounts in Loudoun and Fairfax Counties, including a mandatory 4-hour curtailment for 12 co-location data center operators in the Ashburn Technology Corridor totaling 380 MW of shedding capacity. Transmission voltage on the 500 kV Doubs-Brambleton-Loudoun corridor sagged to 492 kV during the peak, within 3 kV of the lower limit, prompting reactive power dispatch from static VAR compensators at the Brambleton substation that successfully stabilized voltage throughout the Northern Virginia 500 kV network.

An unplanned forced outage at the B.L. England Generating Station in Cape May County, New Jersey removed 447 MW of oil-fired capacity from the PJME-NJ zone due to a turbine bearing failure detected during routine vibration monitoring, occurring during a late-summer heat event with zone demand of 16,800 MW. Atlantic City Electric coordinated emergency energy imports of 280 MW through the Branchburg switching station from PSEG's North Jersey generation portfolio while PJM dispatch increased economic commitment of combined-cycle capacity at the Linden Cogen and Sewaren facilities by 320 MW to compensate for the lost coastal generation. The B.L. England unit was returned to service after 39 hours following emergency bearing replacement by a specialized contractor mobilized from Virginia, with the facility operating under enhanced vibration monitoring protocols for the subsequent 30-day period per NRC-analog guidelines applied by the plant's independent safety review board.

Post-tropical cyclone Michael, weakened from Category 5 intensity, brought 60 mph wind gusts to the Northern Neck and Eastern Shore of Virginia, triggering 19 transmission relay operations on Dominion Energy's 115 kV rural network in Westmoreland, Northumberland, and Lancaster Counties in the PJME-VA zone. The storm-driven outages affected 94,000 customers in rural tidewater Virginia, with Dominion's Northern Neck transmission corridor isolated for 27 hours following a double-circuit tower failure on the Kilmarnock-White Stone 115 kV line caused by a large tulip poplar tree falling across both circuits simultaneously. Emergency mutual aid crews from Appalachian Power and Duke Energy Carolinas supplemented Dominion's restoration workforce, completing all transmission repairs within 44 hours while temporary generation from 78 MW of rental diesel generators maintained power to the Northern Neck Cooperative's critical hospital and public safety loads during the isolation period.

A gas compressor malfunction on the Transco Leidy Line serving eastern Pennsylvania reduced firm gas delivery to PJME-PA-EAST zone generating units by approximately 800 MW of equivalent capacity during a mild autumn period with zone demand of only 8,400 MW, providing sufficient operational headroom to manage the disruption without emergency actions. PECO Energy notified affected gas-fired units at the Schuylkill and Grays Ferry plants, which reduced output by 420 MW combined and were replaced by economic dispatch of additional oil-capable capacity at the Cromby and Eddystone facilities at negligible incremental cost given the low-demand period. Transco repair crews restored normal compressor operation within 18 hours following replacement of a faulty discharge valve assembly at the Mahwah compressor station, and all gas-fired generating units in the PJME-PA-EAST zone returned to full output by 02:30 EST the following morning.

A late-season ice storm deposited 1.1 inches of glaze ice on conductors across central New Jersey, causing protective relay operations on 22 transmission and subtransmission circuits in Middlesex, Somerset, and Hunterdon Counties and triggering a PJME-NJ zone voltage depression of 4 kV below nominal on the 230 kV Camden-Trenton corridor for 90 minutes. JCP&L's system operations team implemented emergency switching to transfer 1,200 MW of load from the impacted northern 230 kV bus to the alternate Edison-Kearny path while PSEG Northern activated reactive power compensation equipment at the Roseland and Sewaren substations to restore voltage to within acceptable limits. Full circuit restoration required 43 hours as crews worked in deteriorating weather to perform ice patrol inspections and replace 14 damaged suspension clamps and 4 vibration dampers on ice-compromised spans along the Bound Brook-Millstone 230 kV corridor in Somerset County.

An anomalously warm pre-Christmas period with temperatures of 72°F in Philadelphia on December 23rd reduced PJME-PA-EAST zone load to 8,100 MW, nearly 3,400 MW below the seasonal forecast, requiring PJM to manage an oversupply condition as must-run nuclear baseload at Limerick and Peach Bottom exceeded minimum system load requirements by 680 MW during the overnight period. Grid operators implemented economic curtailment of non-firm energy imports and issued a manual load shedding advisory to large industrial accounts requesting voluntary production increases to consume surplus generation and maintain system frequency above 59.97 Hz during the low-load overnight window. The unusual warm-December demand profile necessitated real-time rebidding of generation commitment by 14 combined-cycle units across the PJME-PA-EAST and adjacent zones, adding approximately $180,000 in out-of-merit dispatch costs over the two-day holiday anomaly period before temperatures returned to near-seasonal normals on December 25th.

A planned 36-hour outage at the Elroy substation in Bucks County, Pennsylvania for installation of a new 500/230 kV autotransformer to replace a 1974-vintage unit required PJM to place the Hatboro-Elroy 500 kV line on administrative hold and reroute 1,800 MW of normal power flow through the Whitpain-Christiana corridor at elevated loading. PECO Energy's transmission operations team coordinated with PJM to reduce output at the Limerick Nuclear Station Unit 2 by 120 MW as a precautionary measure to reduce thermal loading on the alternate path to within 5% of the normal emergency limit during the projected peak demand window of 11,400 MW. The autotransformer installation was completed successfully within the 36-hour window on August 14th, increasing the Elroy substation's transformation capacity from 900 MVA to 1,350 MVA and eliminating a longstanding thermal constraint that had cost the PJME-PA-EAST zone an estimated $4.2 million annually in congestion charges.

FERC Order 841 compliance requirements mandating electric storage resource participation in PJM's energy and capacity markets prompted the PJME-NJ zone to update interconnection procedures for 14 pending battery energy storage applications totaling 380 MW in Atlantic, Camden, and Burlington Counties, requiring revised power flow studies and protection coordination analyses across the affected 69 kV and 138 kV networks. PSE&G's planned 200 MW lithium-ion battery storage facility in Kearny, Hudson County required amendments to its interconnection agreement to specify state-of-charge management protocols and revised frequency droop response parameters compatible with PJME-NJ operating procedures under the new federal rule. The compliance implementation required 48 hours of coordination between PJM interconnection staff, the NJBPU, and seven independent power producers to finalize pro forma interconnection agreements and update PJM's eDART real-time monitoring system to accept state-of-charge telemetry from enrolled storage resources.

Temperatures reaching 98°F in Wilmington, Delaware combined with oppressive dew points of 78°F produced a heat index of 114°F across northern Delaware and the Delmarva Peninsula, driving PJME-MD zone peak demand to 10,400 MW and triggering activation of 480 MW of Delmarva Power's interruptible commercial load contracts across New Castle and Kent Counties. BGE and Delmarva Power jointly requested PJM to commit three previously uncommitted combustion turbine units at the Perryman and Vienna generating facilities totaling 360 MW of fast-start capacity to maintain adequate reserves as the afternoon peak exceeded the day-ahead energy schedule by 1,600 MW. The heat event persisted for 6 consecutive days with daily peaks above 10,000 MW, accumulating 3,400 MW-hours of demand response activations and $2.8 million in emergency energy costs before a frontal passage on July 4th finally broke the oppressive pattern across the PJME-MD zone.

Hurricane Florence, though weakened to a tropical storm over the Appalachians, delivered 9.8 inches of rainfall to northern Virginia and 8.4 inches to the Maryland suburbs in 18 hours, causing flooding at two 230 kV transmission right-of-way access points in Stafford County, Virginia and triggering an emergency de-energization of the Fredericksburg-Possum Point 230 kV line to prevent structural damage from storm-surge inundation of the Potomac River crossing towers. PJME-VA zone operators rerouted 1,100 MW of normal line flow through the alternate Doubs-Brambleton 500 kV corridor and reduced Dominion Energy's Possum Point generation dispatch by 480 MW to manage thermal loading constraints on the emergency path during the 12-hour isolation period. Full restoration of the Fredericksburg corridor required 41 hours after floodwaters receded, with Dominion transmission crews completing a comprehensive structural inspection of all 34 affected tower foundations along the Rappahannock River crossing before re-energizing the line at 06:30 EST on September 16th.
